key_highlights: Iran war peace talks stall, pushing Brent crude above $110/barrel and fueling a broad energy shock across global markets.
Tech stocks face a selloff amid AI spending fatigue and geopolitical uncertainty, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq mixed.
Defense and gold stocks are gaining attention as safe havens and undervalued plays, with Lockheed Martin, Huntington Ingalls, and several miners highlighted.
The yen weakens after BOJ's Ueda softens hawkish tone, while UK bond yields spike to 5% ahead of BOE meeting.
China blocks Meta's $2B AI acquisition, escalating cross-border tech deal risks.
GM beats earnings and raises guidance on reduced tariff costs from a Supreme Court ruling, offering a bright spot in industrials.
Bank of England warns of potential stock crash as market risks build, urging portfolio protection.
AI fears drive investors to rethink long-term growth bets, with Goldman noting a shift away from mega-cap tech.
Oil shock is seeping into earnings, with Toyota suppliers, Maruti, and Shin-Etsu reporting profit pressure.
Gold retreats slightly but remains elevated as diplomatic efforts to end the Iran war are weighed.

sectors.trend: Strong bullish momentum as Brent crude tops $110/barrel due to stalled Iran peace talks and supply disruption risks, including the first supertanker attempt to exit Hormuz.
sectors.implications: Energy stocks and oil producers (e.g., CNOOC, Tullow) are surging, but downstream sectors like airlines, trucking, and manufacturing face margin compression. Investors should overweight energy but watch for profit-taking if diplomacy resumes.

sectors.trend: Bearish pressure amid AI spending concerns ($600B projected), OpenAI worries sparking selloff, and China blocking Meta's AI deal. Intel downgraded, and Goldman notes investors rethinking long-term AI bets.
sectors.implications: Mega-cap tech (Magnificent 7) earnings this week are critical; any miss could deepen selloff. AI-related stocks are volatile; consider reducing exposure to high-valuation names and rotating to value or defense.

sectors.trend: Positive momentum as geopolitical tensions boost demand. Lockheed Martin, Huntington Ingalls, and StandardAero are flagged as undervalued. GD wins new submarine program.
sectors.implications: Defense spending is likely to increase amid Iran conflict and global instability. These stocks offer both value and growth; consider adding on pullbacks.

sectors.trend: Gold retreats slightly from highs as diplomatic push for Iran peace weighs, but miners like Fortuna, Caledonia, and B2Gold are making strategic moves. Multiple analysts highlight gold miners as buys.
sectors.implications: Gold remains a hedge against inflation and geopolitical risk. Pullbacks are buying opportunities. Focus on producers with strong cost controls and growth projects.

sectors.trend: Mixed: GM beats earnings and raises guidance on tariff relief, but Toyota suppliers and Maruti face profit pressure from Iran war costs. Indian truckers panic over looming diesel hikes.
sectors.implications: Tariff relief benefits some automakers, but oil shock is a headwind. GM is a relative winner; avoid companies with high energy exposure in supply chains.

sectors.trend: Cautious: Euro-zone banks tighten credit standards most since 2023, Barclays reports £66B exposure to non-bank lending, and UK bond yields spike to 5%. BOE warns of stock crash.
sectors.implications: Credit conditions are tightening, which could slow economic growth. Financial stocks may underperform; favor well-capitalized banks with diversified revenue.

sectors.trend: Weak: Claire's closes all UK/Ireland stores (1,300 jobs lost), budget airlines seek $2.5B government aid, and Vinted's valuation drops. Consumer confidence is fragile.
sectors.implications: Discretionary spending is under pressure from inflation and oil shock. Avoid retailers with high leverage; focus on essentials and discounters.

key_themes: Iran war and oil supply disruption driving energy rally and broad economic headwinds
AI spending fatigue and regulatory risks (China blocking Meta deal, OpenAI lawsuit) pressuring tech
Defense and gold as safe-haven plays amid geopolitical uncertainty
Central bank divergence: BOJ dovish, BOE hawkish, ECB noting inflation jump
Credit tightening and financial stability risks (BOE warning, euro-zone bank tightening)
Earnings season revealing winners (GM) and losers (Toyota suppliers, Maruti) from oil shock
Cross-border tech deal risks escalating (Meta/China, SpaceX IPO hype)
Consumer strain from inflation and rising rates (retail closures, airline bailout requests)

risk_assessment.mitigation: Increase energy and defense exposure; reduce cyclical consumer and industrial stocks. Hedge with gold and commodities.
Trim mega-cap tech positions, diversify into value and defensive sectors. Monitor Magnificent 7 earnings closely.

risk_assessment.risk_factor: Central Bank Policy Divergence / Rate Shock
risk_assessment.mitigation: Reduce duration in bond portfolios; favor floating-rate instruments. Hedge currency risk (yen, pound).
Avoid highly leveraged financials and non-bank lenders. Increase cash and high-quality bonds.

risk_assessment.risk_factor: Cross-Border Tech Deal Blockages / Regulatory Risk
risk_assessment.mitigation: Reduce exposure to companies with significant China exposure or pending regulatory approvals. Favor domestic tech.
Rotate from discretionary to staples and healthcare. Monitor retail earnings and job data.

opportunities.recommendation: Overweight energy stocks, especially integrated oil and supermajors with strong dividends.
opportunities.rationale: Brent above $110 and supply disruption risk supports elevated prices. CNOOC, Tullow, and Swiss fund managers see room to run.

opportunities.recommendation: Buy undervalued defense stocks on pullbacks.
opportunities.rationale: Geopolitical tensions and new contract awards (GD submarine) support earnings growth. Lockheed Martin, Huntington Ingalls, and StandardAero are analyst favorites.

opportunities.recommendation: Accumulate gold miners with strong fundamentals and growth projects.
opportunities.rationale: Gold remains a hedge against inflation and geopolitical risk. Pullbacks are buying opportunities. Fortuna, B2Gold, and Caledonia are making strategic moves.

opportunities.recommendation: Consider GM as a value play in industrials.
opportunities.rationale: GM beat earnings and raised guidance due to tariff relief from Supreme Court ruling. Strong execution in a tough environment.

opportunities.recommendation: Invest in high-yield savings or money market accounts for cash reserves.
opportunities.rationale: Rates up to 4.1% APY offer safe returns amid market volatility. BOE warning suggests cash is king.

defensive_moves.recommendation: Reduce exposure to mega-cap tech and AI-related stocks ahead of earnings.
defensive_moves.rationale: AI spending fears and OpenAI worries are driving selloffs. Goldman notes investors rethinking long-term growth bets. Wait for clearer signals.

defensive_moves.recommendation: Hedge against oil shock with short positions in airlines and consumer discretionary.
defensive_moves.rationale: Budget airlines seek bailouts, Claire's closes stores, and Indian truckers panic. Oil above $110 will crush margins.

defensive_moves.recommendation: Increase allocation to short-duration government bonds and TIPS.
defensive_moves.rationale: UK bond yields spiking to 5% and ECB inflation jump signal rate risk. Short-duration protects against further yield increases.

defensive_moves.recommendation: Avoid non-bank lenders and highly leveraged financials.
defensive_moves.rationale: Barclays £66B exposure and euro-zone credit tightening point to stress in shadow banking. Stick with well-capitalized banks.

defensive_moves.recommendation: Build cash reserves and consider put options on broad market indices.
defensive_moves.rationale: BOE warns of stock crash, and multiple risks (Iran, tech, credit) are converging. Cash provides optionality to buy dips.

market_outlook.short_term: Markets will remain volatile with a negative bias over the next 1-3 months. The Iran war and oil prices are the dominant drivers. Tech earnings this week are a key swing factor; any disappointment could trigger a 5-10% correction in the Nasdaq. Defense and energy will outperform. Gold may consolidate but stay elevated. Central bank meetings (BOE, ECB) will add to rate uncertainty. Credit conditions are tightening, raising recession risks.
market_outlook.long_term: Over 6-12 months, the outlook depends on resolution of the Iran conflict. If diplomacy succeeds, oil could fall back to $80-90, relieving pressure on consumers and industrials, and tech could rebound. If war escalates, a global recession becomes more likely, with energy and defense the only safe havens. AI spending will remain a growth theme but with more scrutiny on ROI. The BOE warning of a stock crash underscores systemic risk; investors should maintain defensive positioning.
key_catalysts: US response to Iran proposal and potential Hormuz reopening
Magnificent 7 earnings this week (especially Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet)
BOE and ECB policy decisions in May
US jobs report and CPI data for April
China-Meta deal fallout and cross-border tech regulation
SpaceX IPO hype and retail investor demand
Supreme Court ruling on Bayer Roundup labels
Euro-zone credit tightening impact on corporate borrowing
watch_list: Brent crude oil price and Iran diplomatic developments
S&P 500 and Nasdaq technical levels (support at 5,000 and 15,500 respectively)
US 10-year Treasury yield and UK gilt yield
Gold price and gold miner ETF (GDX)
GM and other auto sector earnings for oil shock impact
Bank of England and ECB forward guidance
Consumer discretionary retail sales and earnings
